segments.s640-g105: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” از دشت نخچیر is a prepositional phrase (from/out of + noun phrase) meaning “from the hunting/plain.” باز آمدند is a past plural verb phrase: باز (adverb “back/again”) precedes آمدند (past/plural “they came/returned”), so the clause reads “when they returned from the hunting plain.”
segments.s640-g106: This is a nominal subject phrase plus the verb: خجسته‌پی و بزمساز are personal names/titles joined by و “and,” and آمدند is the plural past verb agreeing with both names (“they came”). In poetry such descriptive words often form proper names (hence the compounds).
segments.s640-g107: چنین هم بگوی is an exhortation: چنین هم (“thus/so, also”) + بگوی, an imperative/jussive form meaning “say (this).” The following به نخچیر و سور is a prepositional phrase “regarding/at the hunt and the feast” (به + two coordinated nouns). In context the speaker instructs to say the pointed claim about both the hunt and the banquet.
segments.s640-g108: زمانی here functions as a temporal noun/adverb meaning “at any time/ever.” نبودی is the negative past form (ن + بودی) meaning “you were not” or more generally “there was not (a time when).” ز + بهرام دور is a prepositional phrase “distant from Bahram.” Together the clause means “there was no time when you were distant from Bahram.”
segments.s640-g33: همی before the verb gives a durative/progressive sense (“kept/continually”); راند is the past tense verb “drove/led.” The following phrase منزل به منزل modifies the manner/place—“from stage/camp to stage/camp”—and سپاه is the direct object marked by its position after those modifiers. Word order here (adverb + verb + object phrase) is common in epic Persian.
segments.s640-g34: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” زان is the ablative/pronominal adverb “of/ from that,” linking to آگهی “news/information.” یافت is the past of یاب meaning “found/learned,” and the subject بهرامشاه follows the verb (a frequent poetic inversion): “when Bahramshah learned of that.”
segments.s640-g35: بزرگان is the plural subject “the great ones/notables.” ز هر شهر is a prepositional phrase “from each/every city” using the poetic preposition ز. برخاستند is the past plural verb “they rose/rose up,” so the clause reads “Notables from every town rose up.”
segments.s640-g36: پذیره شدن is an infinitival/nominal phrase meaning “the reception / being received” (شدن forming the verbal/ passive notion). را is the definite/direct‑object marker attaching to that phrase, and بیاراستند (past plural) means “they prepared/arranged/ornamented,” so the whole means “they made ready the reception.”
segments.s640-g37: بیامد is the past tense “came” and is placed before its subject شهنشاه (poetic verb‑first order). تا here marks the extent/destination “as far as / to” (تا نهروان = “to Nahrawan/ as far as Nahrawan”), so the clause reads “The king came to Nahrawan.”
segments.s640-g38: A series of adjectives (خردمند و بیدار و روشن‌روان) follows the noun to describe the king: “wise and alert and bright‑souled.” The conjunction و simply links the adjectives in the usual enumerative way.
segments.s640-g39: دو (numeral) + شاه gives “two kings.” گرانمایه and نیک‌ساز are epithets placed after شاه, meaning “esteemed/precious” and “well‑doing/benefactor,” joined by و. This is a noun phrase ‘two noble, beneficent kings.’
segments.s640-g40: رسیدند is the past plural “they arrived.” پس is a connective “then/afterward.” یک به دیگر is a reciprocal phrase “one to the other” formed with the preposition به (to) plus دیگر (other). فراز here is an adverbial/locative sense “up/on high/forward,” giving the sense “they moved up/approached one another.”
segments.s640-g89: وزان (from that) is a compound/poetic prepositional adverb placed before the noun phrase جایگه (place). شد is the simple past third-person singular of شدن ('to become/come to be') used here in the sense 'came to / moved to' (a common poetic usage). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near the king' with به + location (نزدیک) + the noun شاه.
segments.s640-g90: همی (an older/poetic particle) before a verb gives a continuous or habitual nuance ('kept/was continually'). کرد is the past tense of کردن functioning as the main verb 'did/made' and combines with نگاه (a noun 'gaze') to mean 'kept a gaze' or 'kept looking'. مرد is the subject (positioned after the verb in this poetic order) and اندر ایوان is a locative phrase 'in/within the iwan'.
segments.s640-g91: چو here functions as the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones/nobles'. خرم شد(ند) is the plural past 'became joyful'; از نبید is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from the wine'—so the whole clause reads 'when the nobles became merry from the wine.'
segments.s640-g92: پرستار او is a noun + pronoun construction where او after the noun marks possession ('his attendant'). خوابگاهی (the noun خوابگاه with the adjectival/nominalizing suffix -ی) means 'a sleeping-place/quarters'. گزید is the simple past of گزیدن 'to choose', so 'his attendant chose a sleeping-place.'
segments.s640-g93: سوی خوابگه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the sleeping-place' (سوی + place). رفتن is the verbal infinitive/noun 'going' used as the complement of آراستند; آراستند is the past plural 'they adorned/arranged/prepared'. Together this yields 'they made ready for going toward the sleeping-place.'
segments.s640-g94: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from' or 'among'; هرگونه‌ای means 'of every kind' or 'any sort'. جامه‌ها is the plural object 'garments/robes'. خواستند is the past plural of خواستن 'they wanted/asked for' — here 'they requested garments of every kind.'
segments.s640-g95: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. پیدا شد is the idiom 'appeared / became visible' (پیدا + شد, where شد is the past of شدن 'to become' used as auxiliary). این چادر مشک‌رنگ is the noun phrase 'this musk-colored tent' (مشک‌رنگ a compound adjective after the noun), so the clause reads 'when this musk-colored tent appeared.'
segments.s640-g96: This clause is elliptical (no explicit copula); ستاره 'stars' is the topic/subject. بروبر is a literary adverb meaning 'face to face / opposite one another'. چو is the comparative 'like/as', and پشت پلنگ is the simile 'the back of a leopard'. Together they create 'the stars, face to face, like a leopard's back.'
segments.s640-g1: پس is a sentence‑initial temporal/consecutive adverb (‘then’). آگاه شد is a past tense compound meaning ‘became aware’ (شد as the past of شدن). The subject شنگل follows the verb (poetic V–S order). از کار شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘about/of the king’s matter’ with از governing کار (the affair) and شاه in genitive relation.
segments.s640-g2: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the topic ‘concerning’. دختر is the head noun ‘the daughter’. که begins a relative clause ‘who/that’. شد is here a past verb ‘became / was (brought)’. شاه را پیش‌گاه is an idiomatic expression ‘into the shah’s presence’; را marks شاه as the definite target and پیش‌گاه is the place/setting ‘audience / presence’. The whole clause modifies دختر.
segments.s640-g3: به دیدار ایران is a prepositional phrase ‘to/for a visit of Iran’ (به + noun = ‘toward/for’). بدش آرزوی is an inverted idiom: بد modifies آرزوی and ش is the enclitic pronoun ‘his’ (referring back to the subject), so literally ‘an ill/strong desire came upon him’ = ‘he longed/desired strongly’. The order is poetic: the object/desire follows the locative phrase.
segments.s640-g4: بر introduces the object/target of the desire here (‘about/for’). دختر شاه is a genitive construction ‘the king’s daughter’ (دختر modified by شاه). آزاده‌خوی is a postpositive adjective ‘free‑spirited / noble‑minded’ describing the daughter. Together they specify the cause of the desire.
segments.s640-g5: فرستاد is a past transitive verb ‘sent’. هندی is an adjective placed before the noun and indicates origin ‘Indian’. فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase ‘an envoy’ (the -ای ending gives a sense of ‘a certain envoy’); word order (adj + noun) is poetic but normal in classical style.
segments.s640-g6: This is an appositive description of the envoy. سخن‌گوی is a noun/adjective ‘speaker / spokesman’. مردی here is used adjectivally or appositionally ‘a manly/virile man’ (or simply ‘a man’). و links the next adjective. آزاده‌ای is an indefinite adjective ‘a free‑hearted / noble‑minded one’; the repeated indefinite form yields the sense ‘a manly and noble fellow.’
segments.s640-g7: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘a certain one / someone’. عهد نو is the direct object ‘a new covenant/pact’. خواست is a past transitive verb here meaning ‘asked/demanded’. از شهریار indicates the source/target of the request ‘from the king’ (preposition از + noun).
segments.s640-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result (‘so that’ / ‘that’). دارد is present/presumptive ‘has / may have / keeps’ (possessive verb). به خان اندرون is a locative phrase ‘in/at the khan’s inner (quarter)’ with به marking location and اندرون ‘inside’. یادگار is the object ‘a keepsake / memento’ that he intends to have there.
segments.s640-g73: همی + past verb is a literary/historical imperfective: همی رفت = 'he continued/kept going.' The prepositional phrase با خادمان نامدار follows the verb and names the companions (با = with). نامدار is a postpositive adjective modifying خادمان ('renowned attendants').
segments.s640-g74: This clause uses a simple past verb دید ('he saw'). سرای دگر = 'another house/palace' (دگر = 'another')—note that سرای here means 'house/palace' which is not among the given sense options. چون نوبهار is a simile: 'like/as the new spring' (چون = 'like/as', نوبهار = 'new spring'), modifying how the palace appeared.
segments.s640-g75: چو ... دید is a temporal clause meaning 'when he saw.' دخترش را places 'his daughter' as the definite object (را). بر تخت عاج = 'upon an ivory throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). Word order is typical: temporal marker (چو) + object + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s640-g76: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival form 'seated.' به آرام here is an adverbial phrase meaning 'calmly' or 'in a calm manner' (به used adverbially). با فر و تاج = 'with (royal) farr (divine glory) and a crown' — فر is an abstract royal attribute, and و coordinates it with تاج.
segments.s640-g77: بیامد is a simple past 'he came.' The phrase بر سرش بوسه داد is literal: بر سرش = 'upon her head' (بر = on, سرش = her head), بوسه داد = 'gave a kiss' i.e. 'kissed.' داد is the past of دادن used as an auxiliary meaning 'gave' (to give a kiss).
segments.s640-g78: This clause describes placing kisses on her face: رخان را functions as the direct object and is best read here as 'soft/pliant (kisses)' (an adjectival/nominal use), را marks it as definite; به رخسار او = 'on her cheek/face' (به = onto, رخسار = cheek/countenance, او = her). برنهاد is a poetic past 'placed/laid upon.'
segments.s640-g79: پدر is the subject 'the father.' زار بگریست is an idiomatic literary phrase: زار here modifies the verb to mean 'he wailed/bitterly' and بگریست is simple past 'he wept.' از مهر اوی = 'out of his affection for her' (از = 'because of/out of', مهر = 'love/affection', اوی = the possessive/pronominal form referring to 'her').
segments.s640-g80: This verbless nominative clause is typical of classical Persian: همان = 'that same (one/man),' and the following noun phrase بر پدر دختر ماه‌روی identifies him: 'the father of the moon‑faced daughter.' بر here is used in the sense 'of/for' or simply links the apposition; ماه‌روی is a compound adjective 'moon‑faced' describing دختر.
segments.s640-g65: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning ‘was overcome / became wasted’; it stands before the following prepositional phrase. زان = 'from that' (pointing back to the palace), and کاخ شنگل شگفت is a noun phrase (کاخ = palace, شنگل an obscure descriptive epithet, شگفت = ‘marvelous’ used as an adjective). Word order is typical Persian: verb first (poetic inversion) then the modifying phrase.
segments.s640-g66: این عبارت uses a light preposition به + verb construction: به + می‌خوردن (here 'می‌خوردن' means 'to strike / occur to') followed by the object اندیشه 'thought' and the locative/verb گرفت 'seized'. اندیشه (noun) is the thing affected, اندر = 'in/within', and گرفت is simple past 'took / seized'. Together the clause means 'a thought struck/took hold of him.'
segments.s640-g67: که introduces an indirect question/remark ('whether/that'), تا here functions in the sense 'even/so that' introducing the clause 'this is...', and این بهشتست یا بوستان is the copular clause 'this is paradise or (is) a garden.' Word order places the question-word cluster before the predicate, a common classical/poetic pattern.
segments.s640-g68: همی + present verb (آید) is a habitual/continuous marker in classical Persian, here 'kept coming' or 'was coming.' بوی مشک = 'the scent of musk' (bui + noun), از marks the source 'from', and دوستان is the plural 'companions/friends.' The clause is independent but explains the reason for the wonder in the previous segment.
segments.s640-g69: چنین گفت is a formulaic introductory phrase 'thus he said.' با شاه ایران = 'to/with the King of Iran' (با marks the interlocutor), and به راز = 'in secret / in private' (به + راز). The verb sense here is reported speech—this segment sets up what was said next.
segments.s640-g70: که introduces the content clause ('that / saying that'). با دخترم = 'with my daughter' (accompaniment/target of meeting). راه دیدار ساز is an idiomatic request: راه = 'way/means', دیدار = 'meeting', ساز here functions as a verbal/nominalized command 'make/arrange' (classical/poetic use). The whole clause means 'arrange a way to meet with my daughter.'
segments.s640-g71: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that...' and خادمان سپاه is the subject of the subordinate clause (خادمان = attendants/servants, سپاه = host/troops) — word order places the verb of the subordinate clause after this nominal group.
segments.s640-g72: پدر را uses the definite object marker را to mark 'the father' as the object; گذراند (past) = 'caused to pass/led/placed' (he had them move/bring someone). نزدیک ماه = 'near Mah/the Moon' where ماه functions as a locative reference (likely a proper-name epithet). Together: 'he caused the father's (troops) to be brought near Mah.'
segments.s640-g25: This is a nominal coordination: 'همه نامجوی و همه نامدار' = 'all name-seekers and all the famed'. 'همه' (all/everyone) precedes the nouns to quantify them; 'نامجوی' is a compound meaning 'name-seeker' (one who seeks renown). There is no verb here; the clause is nominal and the predication continues in the next segment.
segments.s640-g26: 'همه پاک با طوق و با گوشوار' supplies the predication omitted above: 'all were clean/pure, with collars and with earrings.' 'پاک' functions as a predicate adjective; the copula ('were') is omitted (a normal poetic elision). The repeated 'با' before each item is a poetic stylistic device listing the ornaments.
segments.s640-g27: 'همه ویژه با گوهر و سیم و زر' is an adverbial/attributive phrase: 'همه' (all/each) + 'ویژه' (specially) modifies the following instrument/ornament phrase. 'با' introduces the list of materials/adornments (gems, silver, gold). Word order places the adverbial 'ویژه' before the 'با…' prepositional list for emphasis.
segments.s640-g28: 'یکی چتر هندی ز طاوس نر' continues the description: 'یکی' (= 'one' / 'each a') modifies 'چتر' (canopy/umbrella). 'هندی' qualifies the canopy as 'Indian (style/origin)'. 'ز' is the poetic ablative 'from/of', so 'ز طاوس نر' means 'of (the) male peacock' (i.e., made of peacock — feathers). Again the verb is omitted; this noun phrase is understood as what each person possessed.
segments.s640-g29: 'به دیبا بیاراسته پشت پیل' is a prepositional phrase describing how the elephants appeared: 'به دیبا' = 'in/with brocade', while 'بیاراسته' (here used adjectivally) characterizes the state of the backs ('پشت پیل' = the elephants' backs). The whole phrase functions as an attributive/locative modifier; Persian allows such modifier phrases before the noun they qualify.
segments.s640-g30: 'همی تافت آن لشکر از چند میل' is a finite clause: 'همی تافت' = 'was shining/gleaming' (imperfective/past poetic sense), 'آن لشکر' is the subject ('that host/army'), and 'از چند میل' expresses the extent/measure ('for/several miles'). The verb 'تافت' is past but used poetically to mean 'shone/spread light'.
segments.s640-g31: Here the initial token 'ابا' is a proper name in this context (not the verb 'to refuse'); it stands before a coordinated noun phrase 'هدیه شاه و چندان نثار' = 'the king's gift and so many offerings'. The whole noun phrase describes possessions/tribute associated with Aba. Because the provided senses do not list the proper-name sense for 'ابا', that entry is marked -1.
segments.s640-g32: 'که' introduces a result/explanatory clause: 'that the dinar became khwār (cheap/worthless) before the king.' 'دینار شد' = 'the dinar became', 'خوار بر شهریار' = 'disregarded/cheap in the face of the sovereign' (the preposition 'بر' marks the reference/target — 'for/regarding the shah'). 'شد' is the simple past copular/auxiliary 'became'.
segments.s640-g49: 'به زین بر نشستند هر دو سوار' is a compact, slightly redundant poetic phrasing: به زین بر = 'onto the saddle' (به + زین + بر with two locative/ontop markers), نشستند is the past verb 'they sat/mounted' (the expected نشستن 'to sit' is used here; the supplied lemma/senses do not include this sense), and هر دو سوار = 'both horsemen' (هر functioning as 'each/both' with دو). Word order places the locative phrase and verb before the explicit subject, a common narrative inversion.
segments.s640-g50: همان پرهنر لشکر نامدار is an appositive/descriptive phrase qualifying the warriors: همان = 'that/same', پرهنر = 'skilled', لشکر = 'host/army', نامدار = 'renowned'. In context it describes the very riders/force just mentioned; the three adjectives/nouns sit together as a noun phrase following the clause.
segments.s640-g51: به ایوانها تخت زرین نهاد: به ایوانها is the locative 'to/in the iwans', تخت زرین = 'a/the golden throne' (noun + adjective), and نهاد is the past 'placed/put'. The verb follows its object here (object-verb order) which is common in poetic inversion; نهاد functions as 'they placed'.
segments.s640-g52: برو جامه خسرو آیین نهاد is best read as (بر او) جامهٔ خسرو آیین نهاد — 'upon it they placed Khosrow's ceremonial robe.' Here برو is an archaic/clipped form representing 'بر او' ('upon it') in context; جامه خسرو = 'Khosrow's robe', آیین qualifies the robe as 'ceremonial/ritual', and نهاد again is the past 'placed.'
segments.s640-g53: به + ره is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('به' governing 'ره') giving a directional/locative nuance ('toward/on the way' in a poetic register). "بر" is the preposition 'on/upon' introducing "بره" (lamb) as the surface or base; "مرغ بریان" is a noun phrase 'a roasted bird/fowl' that stands in apposition or is placed upon the lamb. "نهاد" is the simple past (classical narrative past) of نهادن, 'placed/put', and is the main verb that takes the preceding noun-phrases as its objects/locatives.
segments.s640-g54: The phrase "به یک تیر پرتاب" is a prepositional adverbial indicating placement with respect to a single spit/spear (literally 'on/onto one thrown arrow/spear' — a poetic way to name a single skewer/spit). "بر خوان" is the locative 'on/upon the table/banquet', and again "نهاد" is the past 'placed/put'. Word order places the adverbial/instrument phrase before the locative, then finishes with the verb.
segments.s640-g55: "می‌آورد" is the imperfective/present-stem form of آوردن (mi- prefix + آورد) used in narrative to mean 'brought/was bringing' or 'they brought' (habitual/ongoing past in epic style). "و" links the two actions. "برخواند" here is a past/actionive verb meaning 'summoned/called to the meal' (literally 'called to the table'), and "رامشگران" is the direct object — 'those who bring comfort/entertainers/minstrels' — the people called to provide music/comfort.
segments.s640-g56: This is a nominal clause with omitted copula: "همه جام" = 'all the cups'. "پر از کران تا کران" is an adjectival phrase 'full from edge to edge/rim to rim' where "پر" is 'full (of)', "از ... تا ..." marks the extent 'from ... to ...', and "کران" = 'edge/limit'. Together they describe the cups being filled completely.
segments.s640-g9: به نوی جهاندار عهدی نوشت is ordered with the prepositional indirect object first: به + noun phrase ('به نوی جهاندار' = 'to the new sovereign'). 'نوی' is an adjective preceding the head noun 'جهاندار'. 'عهدی' is the direct object (indefinite 'a covenant'), and 'نوشت' is the simple past 3rd-person singular of نوشتن 'to write'. The overall order is topical indirect object — object — verb, a typical classical/poetic SOV grouping.
segments.s640-g10: چو introduces a simile/temporal clause ('چو' = 'as/when/like'). 'خورشید تابان' is a noun + participial adjective ('تابان' = 'shining') following the noun; this adjective is attributive. 'به باغ بهشت' is a prepositional phrase giving the locus ('به' + noun phrase), literally 'to/into the garden of Paradise'. The fragment is thus a simile modifying the action (i.e. he wrote as the sun shines into Paradise).
segments.s640-g11: یکی پهلوی نامه از خط شاه is a compact noun phrase. 'یکی' is the indefinite 'a certain' or 'one'; 'پهلوی نامه' = 'a Pahlavi (i.e. in Pahlavi script) letter' with the language adjective before the noun. 'از خط شاه' is an ablative/genitive-style phrase ('از' + 'خط شاه') meaning 'in the handwriting (script) of the king' — 'از' here marks source/characteristic rather than motion.
segments.s640-g12: فرستاده آورد و بنمود راه contains two coordinated verbal clauses. 'فرستاده' is subject 'the messenger'; 'آورد' is past 3rd singular 'brought' (object 'the letter' is omitted but understood). 'و' links the clauses; 'بنمود' (archaic/polite past of 'نمودن') = 'showed, displayed'; 'راه' is the direct object 'the way/road'. The verb-final clauses follow classical SOV patterns and the prefix/b-prefix in 'بنمود' is an older stylistic form.
segments.s640-g13: فرستاده چون نزد شنگل رسید is a temporal clause introduced by 'چون' ('when/as'). 'فرستاده' is the subject; 'نزد' is a preposition meaning 'near' or 'in the presence of' and governs the following proper name 'شنگل' (the addressee/place). 'رسید' is the past 3rd singular 'arrived/reached' placed at clause end, as expected in Persian past-tense clauses.
segments.s640-g14: سپهدار قنوج خطش بدید is a simple past clause: 'سپهدار' (a title, 'commander') followed by the name/place 'قنوج' (appositional or attributive place-name). 'خطش' is 'خط' + enclitic -ش meaning 'his/its script/handwriting' (referring to the letter's script), and 'بدید' is the archaic past 'saw' (ب‑ + دید). Word order: subject (title + name), object ('خطش'), verb at the end.
segments.s640-g15: ز هندوستان ساز رفتن گرفت uses 'ز' ('from') to mark origin ('from India'). 'ساز رفتن' is a noun + infinitive compound meaning 'preparation for going' or 'the making of plans to go'; such noun+رفتن constructions express purpose/arrangement. 'گرفت' here is past 3rd singular of گرفتن used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'took up/commenced' (i.e. 'began to prepare to go').
segments.s640-g16: ز خویشان چینی نهفتن گرفت similarly begins with 'ز' ('from' / 'among'), followed by the noun phrase 'خویشان چینی' ('Chinese relatives'). 'نهفتن' is the older infinitive 'to hide' (classical alternative to modern پنهان شدن), and with گرفت it has the idiomatic sense 'began to hide' or 'took to hiding'. The clause thus states he set about hiding from/among his Chinese kin.
segments.s640-g17: بیامد is a 3rd-person past verb 'came' placed first (poetic inversion). به درگاه او is a prepositional phrase 'to his court' (به + درگاه + possessive او). هفت شاه is the subject placed after the verb (common in epic Persian: verb or clause first, then the noun phrase identifying who came).
segments.s640-g18: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). آیند is 3rd-person plural (here a narrative/historical-present or subjunctive styling) meaning 'they go/set out/come' and refers back to 'the seven kings'. با رای شنگل is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'at Shengel's counsel' (با + رای where رای = 'plan/counsel'), and به راه means 'on the road' = 'on the march/setting out'.
segments.s640-g19: This is an elliptical listing: یکی شاه کابل = 'one (was) the king of Kabul' where the copula/verb is omitted; دگر هند شاه repeats the pattern for 'another (was) the king of Hind'. Word order places the numeral/indefiner + title + place in apposition.
segments.s640-g20: دگر 'another' introduces the third member of the list. شاه سندل is a noun phrase apposing the name (King Sandal). بشد here is a past/irregular perfective-like verb 'it happened / he came/appeared' used to mean 'arrived' or 'took the field'. با سپاه is an instrumental phrase 'with an army'.
segments.s640-g21: Again an appositive list: دگر شاه مندل = 'another, King Mandal.' که introduces a relative clause 'who...' and بد + نامدار is an adjectival phrase where بد negates/pejorates نامدار; literally 'bad‑famed' = 'ill‑famed/infamous.'
segments.s640-g22: همان نیز ties the next item to the previous one ('likewise/also that one'). جندل here is a name/appellation (treated as a proper name in apposition). که introduces its relative clause; بد کامگار is again an adjectival phrase with بد making کامگار negative, so 'ill‑successful' = 'unsuccessful/unlucky in achieving aims.'
segments.s640-g23: This segment lists belongings/attendants introduced by (archaic/poetic) a preposition: [با/ابا] + adjectives and nouns. ژنده پیلان = 'ragged/old elephants' (ژنده modifying پیلان). و connects items: 'and bells' (زنگ). درای functions as 'possessing/wealthy' or 'riches' — the whole phrase names the paraphernalia that accompany the king(s). The initial form ابا is poetic/variant orthography of با and introduces the list.
segments.s640-g24: یکی introduces a particular item/person ('one' or 'a certain one'). چتر هندی = 'an Indian canopy/umbrella' (چتر = canopy/cover, هندی = 'Indian' as an attributive). به سر بر به پای is a stock phrase 'on the head and on the foot' (prepositional phrases indicating location: به ... سر and بر ... پای both convey 'upon' in this poetic phrasing), describing how the canopy is borne/worn.
segments.s640-g41: "به نزدیک اندر فرود آمدند" is an adverbial + verbal clause: به نزدیک (preposition + noun) functions as 'nearby / toward the near place', اندر is the older preposition 'in / into', and فرود آمدند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural compound verb 'they descended / alighted'. Word order is slightly elevated/poetic — literally 'to near in descent they came'.
segments.s640-g42: که introduces the following clause ('that/and'), and با پوزش و با درود آمدند is a simple past, 3rd-person plural clause: با = 'with', پوزش = 'apology/excuse', و = 'and', درود = 'greeting/salutation', آمدند = 'they came'. The repeated با is rhetorical emphasis ('with apology and with greeting').
segments.s640-g43: گرفتند is the past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they took/seized/embraced'. یکدگر را is the object 'one another' marked by the object-marker را. مر is an archaic/clitic element here (not matching the simple 'me/to me' options) functioning reflexively/emphatically with یکدگر; به بر is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'into the embrace / to the bosom' ('به' + بر). The clause thus literally reads 'they took (each) one another to the bosom.'
segments.s640-g44: This is a noun phrase identifying the pair: دو شاه سرافراز = 'the two exalted / triumphant kings' (adjective after noun, usual Persian order). با تاج و فر is a prepositional phrase 'with crown and (royal) glory'; فر here is the technical Shahnameh term for royal radiance/splendour.
segments.s640-g45: پیاده شده is a past participial/verbal phrase 'having dismounted / being on foot' (شده + participle), modifying لشکر 'the army/host'. از هر دو روی is an adverbial phrase 'from / on both sides' (از = 'from', هر دو = 'both', روی = 'side/surface'), so the clause reads 'the army, dismounted, from both sides.'
segments.s640-g46: جهانی here functions as 'the world' / 'worldwide' (classical adjective used as a noun), سراسر is an adverb/particle 'throughout / entirely', and پر از + noun is the construction 'full of ...' so پر از گفت‌وگوی = 'full of conversation'. Grammatically this is a verbless description: 'the world (was) throughout full of conversation.'
segments.s640-g47: دو شاه و دو لشکر is a coordinated noun phrase 'the two kings and the two armies/hosts'. رسیده is the past participle/verb 'have arrived / have come', and بهم is the adverbial 'to one another / together', so the clause means 'the two kings and the two hosts had come together.'
segments.s640-g48: همی رفت is an archaic continuous past construction: همی (old continuous auxiliary) + رفت (past) = 'kept going / continued'. هرگونه is 'every kind / all sorts', and از بیش و کم is an idiom 'from more and less' meaning 'to varying degrees / more or less'. Together: 'all sorts of (things) continued, in greater and lesser measure.'
segments.s640-g81: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/kept on' placed before the action. دست بر X به دست is an idiomatic cluster: دست بر + object = 'to put one's hand on / seize X', and به دست here indicates 'into (his) hand' or 'holding it'. The verb is implied by the narrative past (he seized/held).
segments.s640-g82: ازان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from that/from there' introducing the locative list. کاخ و ایوان و جای are coordinated places, and نشست is the past verb 'sat / settled' referring back to the subject who seized/held the spoil.
segments.s640-g83: سپینود را uses را as the target/recipient marker (the addressee of the speech). گفت is the past 'said', and اینت is a dialectal/poetic contraction of این است 'this is'; بهشت is the noun 'paradise'. Together: 'He said to Sepinoud, "This is paradise."'
segments.s640-g84: برستی is used here in the sense 'departed / was removed / left' (an intransitive past). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by کاخ 'palace' qualified by بت‌آرای 'idol‑adorned' and the adjective زشت 'ugly'. The clause says the addressee has left that ugly idol‑decked palace.
segments.s640-g85: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/that very'; هدیه‌ها is the plural 'gifts'; را marks the definite object; که introduces the relative clause 'which'; آورده بود is the compound past (آورده + بود) meaning 'had brought' (بود functions as the past auxiliary here).
segments.s640-g86: اگر is the conditional 'if'. بدره is a poetic epithet literally 'full‑moon' used here to mean a moon‑bright beauty (a beloved). و = 'and', تاج = 'crown'. و‌گر is a conjunctive 'or / but if'. برده is the past participle/verb 'taken' (here 'had taken'), and بود again is the auxiliary forming the pluperfect 'had taken'.
segments.s640-g87: بدو is 'to him/unto him' (the indirect object). داد is the past 'gave'. با هدیه means 'with gifts', and شهریار names the king — the line states that he handed someone over/ gave (something) to the shah together with gifts.
segments.s640-g88: شد is the past 'became'. آن خرم ایوان = 'that joyful/pleasant iwan'. چو is the simile 'like/as', باغ بهار literally 'a spring garden' — the clause says the iwan was transformed into a place like a spring garden.
segments.s640-g57: چو (a temporal conjunction) here means "when"; نان خورده شد is a passive/periphrastic past (خورده + شد) meaning "had been eaten"; مجلس شاهوار is a noun phrase with noun first (مجلس "assembly/feast") and postpositive adjective شاهوار "splendid/regal" — literally "when the bread had been eaten, the splendid assembly...".
segments.s640-g58: بیاراست is a stative/past adjectival/verb form meaning "was/were adorned" or "well‑adorned"; پر + noun is the idiom "full (of)" (پر بوی = "full of scent"); the coordinating و joins بوی, رنگ and نگار (scent, color, ornament) as a list of qualities describing the assembly.
segments.s640-g59: پرستندگان (plural noun) "worshippers/devotees" + ایستاده (adjectival/participle) "standing"; the prepositional phrase به پای means "at/near the feet (of)" — typical courtly phrasing meaning they stood at (someone's) feet in homage.
segments.s640-g60: بهشتی شده is a compound of adjective + داشته/شد (become) meaning "has become heavenly/like paradise"; کاخ و گاه و سرای is a three‑item coordination listing grand spaces (palace, place/time, and house/abode) — together describing the royal quarters turned heavenly.
segments.s640-g61: همه آلت is a nominal phrase "all the vessels/utensils"; می‌سراسر is a poetic adverb/particle meaning "throughout/entirely" (intensifying the following quality); بلور is the predicate noun/adjunct "crystal" — together: "all the vessels were wholly of crystal."
segments.s640-g62: طبقهای زرین is a noun phrase "golden platters/trays" (طبق‌های = trays), and the preposition/particle ز (poetic از) introduces the material or cause: ز مشک و بخور means "with/of musk and incense" — i.e. the golden platters (or the air around them) perfumed by musk and incense.
segments.s640-g63: ز زر افسری is a genitive/partitive phrase "a diadem of gold" (ز = poetic از/"of/from"); بر سر is the locative prepositional phrase "on the head of"; میگسار is the noun "cupbearer/wine‑companion" — word order places the description of the ornament before the locative phrase: "a gold diadem on the cupbearer's head."
segments.s640-g64: به پای indicates location "at the feet/for the feet"; اندرون names the inner apartments/within the women's or inner quarters; کفش گوهرنگار is a compound noun phrase "jeweled shoes" (گوهرنگار = jewel‑adorned). Together the segment describes jewel‑studded shoes present at the feet inside the inner chambers.
segments.s640-g97: بکردند is the past tense of کردن used here with a noun phrase to mean 'experienced/fell into'; in classical Persian it often precedes the subject in poetic inversion, so the verb بکردند + the following noun phrase yields 'the drinkers made/experienced a pleasant sleep.' میخوارگان is the plural subject 'the wine-drinkers' and خواب خوش is the nominal complement 'a pleasant sleep.'
segments.s640-g98: همه modifies ناز and 'ناز را' is marked with the object/definiteness marker را. دست کرده is a compound participial phrase from دست‌کردن + کرده meaning 'having put (their) hand on / having taken hold of.' بکش is the verb 'کشیدن' in the sense 'to pull/draw' (here describing what they did after seizing the affectation); the subject (میخوارگان) is carried over from the previous clause (poetic omission of a repeated pronoun).
segments.s640-g99: چنین تا is a linking phrase 'thus until'; پدید آمد is the classical compound for 'appeared' (پدید as the lexical stem + آمد as the past auxiliary), and آن زرد جام is the determiner + adjective + noun ('that yellow cup/goblet') with the adjective and determiner in their expected poetic positions.
segments.s640-g100: که introduces a relative clause modifying جام ('which'). خورشید خوانی uses خوانی as a deverbal noun/inflected form from خواندن meaning 'the calling/that calls' (here sense 'to call/summon'); مر is the older clitic for 'to me' and او را is 'him' marked by را (object). به نام 'by name' indicates the manner of calling. The clause therefore reads roughly 'which the sun called out by name to me (i.e. the sun called him by name to me),' with classical word order and archaic clitic مر.
segments.s640-g101: بینداخت is a past form meaning 'threw' or 'cast' (بی- prefix + انداخت) and functions as the main past verb. آن چادر لاژورد is the noun phrase 'that lapis/azured tent' (لاژورد an adjectival color/stone word placed after the noun in poetic diction).
segments.s640-g102: بگسترد is the past 'spread' (ب- prefix + گستردن) and takes the prepositional phrase بر دشت یاقوت زرد 'upon the plain of (yellow) ruby' as its complement. Note the postnominal color/stone phrase یاقوت زرد ('ruby-yellow' / 'yellow-ruby') is a poetic compound describing the plain.
segments.s640-g103: به نخچیر شد is an idiomatic past expression meaning 'set out to the hunt / went hunting' where شد (became/happened) builds the intransitive result and به نخچیر is the prepositional phrase 'to the chase.' شاه بهرام is the title + name 'King Bahram' (poetic word order sometimes places the verb before the subject), and گرد here indicates movement/turning about or functions as part of the verbal/epithetical phrase describing his activity.
segments.s640-g104: شهنشاه هندوستان را is the object phrase 'the emperor / king of India' marked by را. ببرد is the past form of بردن meaning 'he took / carried off' (here rendered as a completed past action: 'carried off' or 'captured'). The clause therefore reports that he carried off the Indian emperor.

sentences.s640-s53: When they returned from the hunting plain, Khajaste‑pi and Bazmsaz came.
sentences.s640-s54: Say likewise this: there was never a time when you were far from Bahram, whether at the hunt or at the feast.
sentences.s640-s17: He drove the army from camp to camp when King Bahram learned that news.
sentences.s640-s18: Notables from every town rose up and prepared the reception.
sentences.s640-s19: The king came to Nahrawan—wise, alert, and bright‑souled.
sentences.s640-s20: The two noble and beneficent kings arrived; then they approached one another.
sentences.s640-s45: From that spot he moved close to the king; the man kept gazing from inside the iwan.
sentences.s640-s46: When the nobles were made merry by the wine, his attendant chose a sleeping-place.
sentences.s640-s47: They prepared to go toward the sleeping-place; they asked for garments of every sort.
sentences.s640-s48: When this musk-colored tent appeared, the stars stood opposite one another like the back of a leopard.
sentences.s640-s1: Then Shengel became aware of the king's affair concerning the daughter who had been brought into the king's presence.
sentences.s640-s2: He was seized by a (strong) desire to go and see Iran on account of the king’s free‑spirited daughter.
sentences.s640-s3: He sent an Indian envoy, an eloquent man — a manly and noble one.
sentences.s640-s4: A certain man requested a new covenant from the king, that he might keep a memento in the inner chamber of the khan.
sentences.s640-s37: He went on with his renowned attendants and saw another palace like the new spring.
sentences.s640-s38: When he saw his daughter seated on an ivory throne, calm and resplendent with royal glory and a crown.
sentences.s640-s39: The father came, kissed her on the head, and laid soft kisses upon her cheek.
sentences.s640-s40: The father wept bitterly from his love for her; that same man was the father of the moon‑faced daughter.
sentences.s640-s33: He was overcome by that marvelous, tangled palace; a thought seized him.
sentences.s640-s34: He wondered whether this was paradise or a garden; the scent of musk kept coming from the companions.
sentences.s640-s35: Thus he told the King of Iran in private: 'Arrange a way for me to meet with my daughter.'
sentences.s640-s36: He ordered that his attendants bring the father's troops up close to Mah.
sentences.s640-s13: All who sought a name and all the renowned — all were cleanly dressed, wearing collars and earrings.
sentences.s640-s14: All specially bedecked with gems, silver and gold; each had an Indian canopy made from the male peacock.
sentences.s640-s15: With brocade-decked backs on the elephants, that host glittered for several miles.
sentences.s640-s16: Aba (bearing) the king's gift and so many offerings that the dinar became cheap before the sovereign.
sentences.s640-s25: Both horsemen mounted their saddles — the same renowned, highly skilled host.
sentences.s640-s26: They placed a golden throne in the iwans and laid Khosrow's ceremonial robe upon it.
sentences.s640-s27: They set a lamb with a roasted fowl upon it, and, skewered on a single spit, placed it on the table.
sentences.s640-s28: They brought it in and summoned the entertainers to the table; all the cups were full from rim to rim.
sentences.s640-s5: He wrote a new covenant to the new sovereign, like the sun shining into the garden of Paradise.
sentences.s640-s6: A Pahlavi letter in the king's own hand was brought by a messenger and displayed the way.
sentences.s640-s7: When the messenger reached Shangal, the commander Qanūj saw the (letter's) handwriting.
sentences.s640-s8: He began preparing to depart from India; he began to hide from his Chinese kinsmen.
sentences.s640-s9: Seven kings came to his court, who set out on the road at Shengel's counsel.
sentences.s640-s10: One was the king of Kabul, another the king of Hind, and another, King Sandal, came with an army.
sentences.s640-s11: Another was King Mandal, who was ill‑famed; likewise Jandal, who was unsuccessful.
sentences.s640-s12: With ragged elephants, bells, and wealth, and one (of them) an Indian canopy — upon his head and upon his feet.
sentences.s640-s21: They came down nearby, and approached with apologies and salutations.
sentences.s640-s22: They took one another in their arms — the two triumphant kings, with crown and royal glory.
sentences.s640-s23: The troops had dismounted on both sides, and the whole world was entirely full of conversation.
sentences.s640-s24: The two kings and the two hosts had come together; all manner of things, from more to less, continued.
sentences.s640-s41: He kept his hand on Shangal's spoil, and, taking it in hand, sat down in that palace, the iwan, and that place.
sentences.s640-s42: He said to Sepinoud, "This indeed is paradise; you have departed from that ugly, idol‑adorned palace."
sentences.s640-s43: Those very gifts which he had brought — if he had taken the moon‑bright (beauty) and the crown, and had carried (her) off.
sentences.s640-s44: He gave him to the king with gifts; that joyful iwan became like a spring garden.
sentences.s640-s29: When the bread had been eaten, the splendid assembly was adorned, full of scent, color, and ornament.
sentences.s640-s30: Worshippers stood at his feet; the palace, the hall, and the dwelling had become heavenly.
sentences.s640-s31: All the vessels were entirely crystal; golden platters scented with musk and incense.
sentences.s640-s32: A golden diadem on the cupbearer's head; in the inner apartments, jewel‑adorned shoes at the feet.
sentences.s640-s49: The wine-drinkers fell into a pleasant sleep; having taken hold of every affectation, they pulled it away.
sentences.s640-s50: Thus, until that yellow goblet appeared — which the sun called out by name to me.
sentences.s640-s51: He cast down that lapis-blue tent and spread it over the yellow-ruby plain.
sentences.s640-s52: King Bahram went out on the hunt and carried off the emperor of Hindustan.
